the cycle of incompetence in a struggling team. And so in order to understand what the cycle looks like, you have to first understand what a struggling team looks like or what one would consider that to be, right? So I want to go over the top five characteristics of a struggling team, okay? Number one and most obvious to an outside eye, as well as really common is just void of vision. And so what I mean by void of vision is not only void of, you don't have a mission, you don't have values, you do not have an overall goal of the business, right? Like they don't understand the purpose of the business. But what goes hand in hand with that is because you don't have an overall overreach, over arching goal, you don't have monthly goals, quarterly goals, weekly goals, right? And so what happens then is that people have no idea how to prioritize the tasks given on their plate. And that's when you see a CEO looking at its staff going, what the heck, why are you guys working on this? This is such a waste of time, it's not a priority. And that's because you did not set proper goals for those people, right? Second is leaders deflecting responsibility. And so this could be your operations manager, head coach, a sales manager, basically a person who is blaming everything on the team, right? And the fact of the matter is that when people point out what's wrong with the team consistently, it's usually because they know deep down there's something wrong with them, right? And they know it's their fault and they just need to be told that, right? We usually point out in others what we don't like about ourselves. So somebody's talking about how the team is always late, the team is always late, the team is always late. They are probably always late. So it's instead of looking at the team as a whole, looking at the leader, something I say a lot is like, if you don't trust your team, then I do not trust you because you built the team, right? Third is the team resisting accountability. And basically what that means is people who are constantly pushing and pushing and pushing everything on outside circumstance. This could be anything from like a Leads or Week, people aren't picking up their phones, you know, customers don't have enough money, like that's all outside circumstance. And those are circumstances that every business has. It doesn't matter if you're a gym or not, everybody gets those things, like the same stuff from sales, customer service, etc. Like it doesn't matter what business you're in, those are always outside circumstances, right? And so we never blame those. Fourth is avoidance of conflict. And so this is also a very common one, especially in a new business because people don't trust each other as much yet. Is you walk into a meeting and you're asking questions and your team is just silent, right? And I remember in the beginning when we first started the business, you know, a couple of our first maybe two quarterly meetings, I felt like nobody was contributing. Look, you know, fast forward a year and a half and everyone talks and I don't say a word. So that is something that happens in the beginning. However, it takes intentional building of trust to ensure that people can then feel comfortable enough to voice their opinions, especially if it's an opinion that contradicts what the owner or a leader is saying. And then last is withholding of trust, basically meaning that you're not giving it and you're also not receiving it. And so what that looks like is you might think that you have entrusted something to someone or that you were showing trust, but not everyone receives trust the same way. So it requires a lot of open dialogue and especially when somebody's new or somebody's just come on the team or you've made a lot of changes, you have to make sure that you're always extending trust before asking for it. So that being said, what does this look like in a cycle? Okay, this is what typically happens in a business. Okay, someone does something wrong. You do not confront the fact that they do anything wrong, nor do they. Blame is casted elsewhere, meaning probably external circumstances, the software, the customer, what the frick ever, right? And then negative assumptions are made. The negative assumptions aren't even ones that you might be voicing, but they are just underlying thoughts that appear in our heads, right? It's like you start to assume that this person is lazy, you start to assume that this person doesn't know what they're doing, you start to assume and you start to assume a lot of things because you haven't confronted it. And so you're actually angry at yourself because you didn't confront it. And so you start assuming negative things about that person rather than confronting the fact that you're not confronting them for a wrong action. I hope that makes sense. It all stems back to you, ultimately, but you have to understand what this looks like, right? And you also have to understand that if this is what it looks like, then what do you have to do to break it, right? So how do you break this cycle? How do you break the cycle of incompetence? When someone produces a wrong action, you must confront them. That is the only way people learn. People are not going to... They do not always know that they've done something wrong and they do not always know how detrimental it could be to the business. And so it is your job to tell people when they've done things that are wrong, right? And that is productive. That is a productive use of your time. Once you've done that, the team can then accept responsibility for what they've done wrong because now they see, oh, I understand that this was the expectation and I fell short here. I accept responsibility for that and next time I'll do better. From there, there are positive assumptions because the person understands, okay, I did something wrong. This is what I need to do next time. You understand that the person didn't even know most of the time they were doing something wrong or you figured out the underlying reason. And now you can produce a new action, a productive one that moves the ball forward, right? And so what it starts with is the confrontation, okay? So to remember it, to break this cycle, the biggest thing that you have to do is confront people, okay? And it's the hardest thing for most people to do, but it's the absolute most necessary. So in breaking the cycle, what you want to think about are four things. One, you have to diagnose the root of the dysfunction, meaning you can't change what you can't define, right? And so as a leader, you have to accept that you're always somehow the root cause. So I'll give you an example is that a lot of the times when somebody is not meeting my expectation and I'm thinking to myself, what the frick do I pay this person? Yeah, I mean, I go through those thoughts like, what am I paying this person for? Like, I literally am paying you and feeling more stressed. And I sit there and I'm like, Layla, do you actually feel that way or do you know that it's your fault? And I'm like, I go back and I think when I hired this person, did I put them through the proper hiring process? Did they go through all of the interviews? Did I try to fast track the process? Was I very clear when I onboarded them? Did I baby them when I onboarded them? Okay, I can tell you there's been a few instances where I expect a lot of someone, but in the beginning I've babyed them, meaning I've helped them too much. And then later on, when I expect they should be doing it on their own, they're like, well, hey, you helped me in the beginning. Why would you not be helping me now? And I'm like, oh, I need to set new expectations because I have set expectations through my actions that it's okay to lean on me this much. And now I have to set new expectations verbally and through my actions. So always looking back, like it is ultimately every problem in your business relates back to you. So you have to figure out what did you do the root cause and then you can use that to figure out how to actually fix it going forward. Okay. Two is that you can't correct behavior that you're not going to confront. Okay. You are addressing behavior. You're not addressing a person. Okay. And so a lot of people take this very, very personally, or they think that others will take it personally. And if you want a culture that's going to produce an effective business, a business that's profitable business that makes money, then you have to produce a culture that is not afraid to speak the truth. It plays that people are not afraid to give and take feedback. And so what that means is that that starts with you and you have to be the ultimate truth teller of everybody. Three is giving the team or the individual a path to perform. So it means once you've told them what they're not doing correctly, you have to go back and say, this is how you go forward from here. So giving them very clear instructions, you know, from the beginning, a lot of times it's like you want to trust people, but once they mess up, if they mess something up, you have to tell them exactly how to correct it. Because clearly they didn't have the same perspective as you from the beginning. So you have to give them very meticulous instruction of how they should go about this situation next time. Right. Four is you want to celebrate when they improve or let them go and they don't. And so what I mean by that is you need to remember that people want to be appreciated. They want praise. And so if you've corrected someone and they probably are feeling down on themselves, you know, most people don't as much as they can take the criticism. It's not fun. Right. And so if they do improve, you have to make sure that you give them kudos. You recognize them for that. Otherwise they're going to assume they're still failing. Okay. People will just assume until you tell them otherwise. So it's very important you communicate that. And then second is that if they do not improve and you've given them all the help and all the coaching and told them exactly what to do, then you need to let them go. Why do you have to do that? Because you cannot tolerate mediocrity in your business. And I'm telling you, like your culture and your business are the result of your level of tolerance. Okay. So it's very important that you abide by your, your words and you stay by your values. And so when somebody is not living up to your standards, you cannot keep them on the team. It will ruin the morale of the rest of your team and it will ruin the business ultimately. So what you need to remember from this is that the most powerful tool that you have in leading your team is your personal example. They are watching everything you do. They hear everything you say amplified, right? Every single thing you do, they watch all of your social media. They watch all of your Instagram stories. They listen to every single thing you say in a meeting, every little sentence you write in an email. It's all amplified. Okay. So you have to remember everything you're doing is under a microscope and cast through a microphone. Okay. And so leading by personal example and always reaching to do your best and when your best isn't enough learning and reaching and doing more and asking for help. That is the best example you can set through your team. Okay. However, what is it that most leaders actually struggle with in this cycle? It is the confrontation. And I want to just hammer this home because if there's one thing I want you to get out of this presentation, it's that you have to confront people. And the first time your hands might be sweating and you might be freaking out and you might feel like a piece of crap. That's okay. As the business owner, you're going to feel like a piece of crap more than most people. That is the life you signed up for. But you must confront people. Okay. If you think about it, the best teachers and mentors you have in life are not those who just want you to like them all the time. They're the people who teach you the hard lessons. They confront you. They call you on your bullshit. Okay. You want to be that person for your team. You have to be that person for your team. Okay. So you need to stop interpreting confrontation as something hostile or negative and simply viewing it as telling the truth. Okay. Don't sugarcoat it. Tell the truth. Make that a part of your culture. That's why our third core tenant is don't sugarcoat it because anytime we go to deliver feedback, we're like, hey, you know, I'm not going to sugarcoat it because we don't do that here. Okay. So some easy icebreakers for these conversations that I want to touch on. And this is how I lead into giving feedback. And I do suggest that you write these things down because it's a lot easier to give feedback when it's been rehearsed. I know that sounds crazy, but until you've done it enough times and it's like swinging a bat, you've just got the motion down, like you need to practice really. So first line is what I want to tell you may not be easy to hear, but I'm telling you because I really value and care about you. Okay. So you're telling the person it's something that's hard, but you value and care about them. Therefore, you need to let them know, you know, you guys, you have to remember you're looking up for them and not just as an employee of yours, but as a person. You always have to remember like as a person, you need to help them grow as a person, not just as an employee of your business. And this is how you do it. A second way you could say it is, you know, I take your growth to heart as well as my role as your boss. And because of that, it's my responsibility to tell you the things even when I don't want to. Okay. So again, letting them know exactly how you feel is the easiest way to open up the conversation. I say that stuff all the time, like, hey, I really need to talk to you, you know, I have your best interest in mind. And I absolutely hate telling you the hard things, but this is going to be one of those hard conversations. It's just what you got to do. So what I want to leave you guys with is one, very few people are naturally confrontational. Okay. However, most people don't want to do want to identify with being honest. Okay. So most people are like, I'm not confrontational, but then everybody in the freaking world wants to be like, I'm an honest person. Okay. Well, being honest goes hand in hand with being confrontational. It's just the fact that people like to most people do not want to be revealed the truth about themselves. And so a lot of people would rather stay ignorant. And so sometimes it's known as being rude to tell the truth. It's not being rude. You're telling the truth. Simple as that. Two is to remember it takes practice. Like I said, doing role play, writing it down, making sure you at least have notes with you before you have these conversations. And then knowing it will be uncomfortable, but it will be easier time. You know, the first time I fired someone, I like cry my eyes out after. And here we are, however many years later, and it doesn't bother me anymore because I know I'm making a decision for the best of the business. Right. And so you have to always keep the long game in mind. Remember, like if you're doing this stuff for the first time, it's okay if you're shaky, it's okay if you're nervous, it's okay if you're sweating. It's just important that you do it. Okay. And so lastly, here are some questions to ask yourself to figure out the status of your team currently. One, on a scale of one to 10, how healthy is your team? So looking at the qualities of a startling team, where does your team fall in that spectrum? Right. One to 10. Two is what is the quality weakest on your team? Is it that people avoid confrontation? Is it that people blame outside circumstances? What is the characteristic of your team? Because it's like a living organism, right? That you think is weakest and it's most vulnerable to. What is the cause of the weakness? Most likely you. And then what are you going to do to address this? And the one thing I want to say is that if you want to address something that's a part of your culture, that you need to change, you need to take 100% ownership and be transparent about it. Meaning, hey, I know that up until now, this is how we've done things. I realized that that is because of me and that's how I started this business. And it's something that's been embedded in our culture. And I've realized and grown and realized that I made that a part of our culture out of inexperience to be frank. And now that I have more experience and knowledge, I realized that this is how we need to operate. And so I want to make that a new expectation of everyone on the team. And then you open up for questions. But end of the day, your self-awareness is going to be what will help you unveil the why your team is struggling. And so it's really important that you ask yourself these questions and that you confront the issues.
